coredata.dc:title: CDs-ICG@BSA nanoparticles for excellent phototherapy and in situ bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Xianju
Cai, Qingchi
Zhao, Shouchun
Ling, Faling
Xiang, Guotao
Li, Li
Wang, Yongjie
Li, Yanhong
Tang, Xiao
coredata.dc:description: For the diagnosis and treatment of cancer, a great challenge is the fabrication of straightforward, non-toxic, multifunctional green nanomaterials. In this study, carbon quantum dots self-assembled with indocyanine green dye at bovine serum albumin for phototherapy and in situ bioimaging are produced by a flexible hydrothermal method. We find that the synthesized nanoparticles have high tumor photothermal therapeutic activity when exposed to 808 nm light, with a photothermal conversion efficiency up to 61 %. The phototoxicity study revealed the excellent phototherapy of the nanoparticles mainly arises from photothermal therapeutic effect other than photodynamic therapy effect. Simultaneously, it allows biological imaging in the visible and near-infrared ranges because of the significant absorption at 365 nm and 840 nm. The current work offers a simple, environmentally friendly, and reasonable method for developing photothermal drugs with a high photothermal conversion efficiency in the near-infrared region, as well as good biosafety for multifunctional nanomaterials for bioimaging tumor diagnosis and direct phototherapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
ICG
Bioimaging
Phototherapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and biocapacity test of CDs-ICG@BSA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: construction of double city economic circle in Chengdu Chongqing region scientific and technological innovation project
National Natural Science Foundation of China

Natural Science Foundation Project of Chongqing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 11704054 , 12004062 ); Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing ( KJZD-M202000601 , KJQN202100615 ), and the construction of double city economic circle in Chengdu Chongqing region scientific and technological innovation project ( KJCX2020028 ).

This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (11704054,12004062); Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (KJZD-M202000601, KJQN202100615), and the construction of double city economic circle in Chengdu Chongqing region scientific and technological innovation project (KJCX2020028).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption spectra of CDs, ICG, CDs-ICG@BSA, insets are the photographs of these solutions under natural light; (b) Absorption, excitation and emission spectra of CDs, insets left are CDs under natural light and right are CDs under 365 nm UV lamp; (c) Excitation and emission spectra of CDs-ICG@BSA, insets are CDs-ICG@BSA solution under natural light and 365 nm UV lamp; (d) Luminescence decay curves of CDs and CDs-ICG@BSA.

(a) Absorption spectra of CDs-ICG@BSA with different contents of BSA. (b) The absorption intensity of ICG versus BSA concentration; (c) Photothermal profiles of the samples with various BSA concentrations under 808 nm laser irradiation (1 W cm−2).

(a) Emission spectra of CDs-ICG@BSA. (b) Relative emission intensities versus the concentration of BSA in the range of 0–8 mg/ml (c)Normalized emission spectra of CDs-ICG@BSA with various BSA concentrations. (d) The emission center (wavelength) versus BSA concentrations.

(a) Absorption spectra of CDs-ICG@BSA solutions with different contents of ICG. (b) Photothermal profiles of H2O, BSA, CDs, CDs-BSA and CDs-ICG@BSA solutions. (c) Thermographic images of CDs-ICG@BSA at t = 0, and t = 300 s. (d) Photothermal profiles of CDs-ICG@BSA (6 mg/mL) solutions under 808 nm laser irradiation at different laser power densities. (e) Heating and cooling curves of CDs-ICG@BSA (6 mg/ml) under 808 nm laser irradiation (1 W cm−2) and linear time during cooling versus -lnθ (the definition of Θ is attached in SI); (f) CDs-ICG@BSA (6 mg/ml) under 808 nm laser irradiation for six consecutive cycles of heating-cooling followed by photothermal stability.

Confocal fluorescence images of 4T1 cells labeled with CDs-ICG@BSA excited at 408 nm: (a/d) bright field, (b/e) dark field, (c/f) overlay field; Confocal fluorescence images of 4T1 cells labeled with CDs-ICG@BSA at excited 640 nm: (h/k) bright field, (i/l) dark field, (j/m) overlay field.

(a) Cell survival of CDs-ICG@BSA irradiated by an 808 nm laser at a power density of 1.0 W cm−2 for 10 min in a dark environment. (b) Cell survival under ROS scavenger (Vitamin C 25 mM) treatment. (c) Cell survival under ice bath treatment. (d) Confocal images of CDs-ICG@BSA incubated 4T1 cells before an 808 nm laser (1.0 W cm−2) irradiation in a dark environment. (e) after an 808 nm laser irradiation for 10 min in a dark environment.

head.ce:title: CDs-ICG@BSA nanoparticles for excellent phototherapy and in situ bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing, 400065, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing, 400065, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. 2#Chongwen Road, Nan'an District, Chongqing, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the diagnosis and treatment of cancer, a great challenge is the fabrication of straightforward, non-toxic, multifunctional green nanomaterials. In this study, carbon quantum dots self-assembled with indocyanine green dye at bovine serum albumin for phototherapy and in situ bioimaging are produced by a flexible hydrothermal method. We find that the synthesized nanoparticles have high tumor photothermal therapeutic activity when exposed to 808 nm light, with a photothermal conversion efficiency up to 61 %. The phototoxicity study revealed the excellent phototherapy of the nanoparticles mainly arises from photothermal therapeutic effect other than photodynamic therapy effect. Simultaneously, it allows biological imaging in the visible and near-infrared ranges because of the significant absorption at 365 nm and 840 nm. The current work offers a simple, environmentally friendly, and reasonable method for developing photothermal drugs with a high photothermal conversion efficiency in the near-infrared region, as well as good biosafety for multifunctional nanomaterials for bioimaging tumor diagnosis and direct phototherapy.

CDs-ICG@BSA nanoparticles have been synthesized by hydrothermal method. The nanoparticles have the advantages of strong photostability, good water solubility, low toxicity and extremely small in size, with excellent photothermal effect and PCE as high as 61 %. Furthermore, CDs-ICG@BSA can imagine the cells during phototherapy.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the enormous incidence and high mortality, leading to millions of deaths annually throughout the world, malignant tumors are regarded as one of the most terrible hazards to human health [1]. The surgical resection, radiation, and chemotherapy are the most popular cancer treatment strategies in the past few decades. However, these treatments cause new damages, so there is an urgent requirement for new techniques with fewer adverse effects [2]. Phototherapy has recently gained considerable interest as an effective approach for oncology treatment because of the non-invasive, practical, low toxicity, and non-resistance [3–5]. Phototherapy includes photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT), which efficiently transforms NIR light into heat and generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) that is phototoxic and can be used to treat tumors. PTT utilizes photothermal agents (PTAs) to locally raise the temperature under the irradiation of near-infrared (NIR) light, which causes tumor cells to undergo apoptosis [6,7]. The secret to PTT is that PTAs efficiently transform the absorbed light into heat, which in turn leads to the tumor site to become extremely hot to injure the tumor cells. Massive PTAs have been explored, such as polyaniline [8], metal chalcogenides [9], noble metals [10], semiconductors [11] and carbon-based nanomaterials [12] etc.

PTAs have been effectively investigated for decades, but there are still challenges required to be resolved in this area. The first is that the PTAs which are now in use, such as metal sulfides and gold-containing nanomaterials, may be hazardous to human and have poor dispersion and metabolism. Organic PTAs generally possess greater biocompatibility and biosafety than inorganic materials, but their applications are constrained by low photostability, difficult synthesis, and high cost. Another issue is the long-term biosafety of nanoscale PTAs in vivo [13]. It has been shown that renal metabolism may eliminate tiny nanoparticles (5–7 nm) [14], reducing their cumulation and long-term biotoxicity in the reticuloendothelial system [15]. Therefore, it is of great importance to produce ultra-small-sized kidney-clearable PTAs.

Owing to their excellent water solubility, minimal toxicity, and exceptional fluorescence properties, carbon quantum dots (CDs) with a size of less than 10 nm are gaining continuous attention as a promising new strategy for tumor PTA and fluorescent imaging [16,17]. In particular, CDs are promising materials for biomedical applications due to the ultra-small particle size, which may be quickly eliminated from the body through renal metabolism. CDs could be potentially served as PTAs because the significant molecular vibrations in nanoscale as nonradiative relaxation convert light into heat when CDs are exposed to photons which are easily absorbed by CDs with abundant loosely bounded electrons in the ground state [10]. But the low photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) is a big challenge. Doping CDs with metal ions like Ni2+ and Er3+ to increase their functionality for cancer detection and treatment has shown to be an appealing method [18]. However, metal ions have the potential hazard. There is still a pressing need for non-toxic PTAs with significant absorbance in NIR region and a high PCE of PTT.

Indocyanine green (ICG) is a biocompatible, photothermally stable dye for in vivo cardiovascular and ocular imaging, visceral tracking, and ophthalmic angiography. It is the only NIR fluorescent dye that has been given the go-ahead for usage in humans by both the European Medicines Agency and the US Food and Drug Administration [19]. ICG is regarded as a good in vivo contrast agent because of its NIR absorption at about 840 nm [20]. More intriguingly, ICG has both PTT and PDT effects, which allows it as an excellent phototherapy agent [21,22]. However, ICG tends to aggregate and become unstable in aqueous solutions [23], stabilizers are necessary to help ICG be stable in water and enhance the phototherapeutic effect in the cellular environment. Bovine serum albumin (BSA), which has prominent tumor cell targeting ability and water solubility, is often added to PTAs as an effective stabilizer [24,25]. Better performance of ICG by introducing BSA has been demonstrated in biomedical applications recently [26,27].

In this study, we developed CDs self-assembly ICG at BSA (CDs-ICG@BSA) nanoparticles as PTAs with efficient NIR absorption that can be used for tumor photothermal/photodynamic therapy and bioimaging in NIR range. CDs-ICG@BSA are created through a hydrothermal process and possess fascinating benefits. CDs-ICG@BSA exhibits great NIR absorption capacity, prominent PCE (61 %), and good photostability under NIR laser irradiation. At the same time, CDs-ICG@BSA is used for biological imaging with the advantages of superior water solubility, minimal cell toxicity, and biocompatibility. So, multifunctional bio-plat with high PCE and on-line biological imaging CDs-ICG@BSA is realized in this study.

Citrate (C6H8O7, CA), diethylenetriamine (C4H13N3) and ethanol (C2H5OH) were purchased from Chuandong Chemical Company. Indocyanine Green (C46H50ClN2NaO6S2, ICG), N, N-dimethylformamide (C3H7NO, DMF), ethylacetate (C4H8O2), petroleum ether (spectral purity) and Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and biocapacity test of CDs-ICG@BSA
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized through a solvothermal method, whose detailed process is included in our previous work [28]. ICG and BSA were dissolved into 2 ml H2O to get Solution A. CDs was dissolved in 10 ml H2O in another beaker to get Solution B. Solution A was then mixed with Solution B followed by continuous and vigorous stirring. After 24 h, ICG loaded CDs was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and washed 3 times by H2O. The CDs-ICG@BSA nanoparticles were obtained by the treatment of lyophilization.
In vitro cytotoxicity test, cell uptake, and photothermal conversion efficiency of CDs-ICG@BSA were carried out at room temperature [29,30]. The elaborate information is found in SI.

The sizes and morphologies of CDs and CDs-ICG@BSA nanoparticles were analyzed by a High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) with the instrument of JEOL JEM-2100F at 200 kV. The absorption of the samples was measured on a Cary 5000 ultraviolet–visible–near infrared spectrophotometer. Cary 630 FTIR spectrometer was employed to record the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in the range of 4000-500 cm−1. The photoluminescence excitation (PLE), photoluminescence (PL) spectra and luminescence lifetime were recorded by an FLS1000 spectrofluorometer. The width of the excitation and emission slits were set as 0.8 nm. The scan interval and integration time were 0.5 nm and 0.2 s, respectively. Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (CLSM) images were obtained by ZEISS Laser Scanning Microscope (LSM880). For all the solution samples characterized in this work, water is the only solvent.

To explore the morphologies and structural alterations in CDs and CDs-ICG@BSA, TEM and HRTEM measurements have been carried out. Fig. S1 shows the corresponding images of CDs in (a)-(c), and CDs-ICG@BSA in (d)-(f), respectively. Both CDs and CDs-ICG@BSA exhibit small, quasi-spherical nanoparticles as their morphology. The photographs in (a) and (d) are TEM images of uniformly tiny particles of CDs and CDs-ICG@BSA with uniform lattice structure. Because these lattice fringes are commensurate with the (100) lattice spacing of graphene along the [001] direction [31], it illustrates that CDs and CDs-ICG@BSA have crystalline carbon structure. The planar spacing of CDs and CDs-ICG@BSA is 0.23 nm and 0.31 nm, as shown in the inset of the figures, respectively. Figs. S1(b) and (e), which represent the HRTEM images of CDs and CDs-ICG@BSA, indicate that the nanostructures in the carbon quantum dots exist as quasi-spherical particles and both have distinct lattice stripes. Figs. S1(c) and (f) display the separated CD and CDs-ICG@BSA particles, which clearly exhibit that ICG is present in the form of encapsulated CDs. The particle size distribution and average particle size of CDs and CDs-ICG@BSA are displayed in Fig. S2 in SI, with average particle sizes of 3.58 nm and 4.29 nm, respectively. It is seen that the self-assembly of ICG to CDs leads to enlarge the particle size and lattice spacing of the nanoparticles, but the quasi-spherical morphology is maintained after ICG loading. Compared to the size of other PTAs used in phototherapy, it is worth noting that the size of as-prepared CDs-ICG@BSA in this study is in the ideal range for nanomaterials which may easily accumulate in tumor tissues [32].

As shown in Fig. S3, the functional groups were examined by FT-IR spectra in order to further understand the chemical composition in CDs, and CDs-ICG@BSA. Stretching vibrations of the following are observed: OH (3459 cm−1), –NH (2944 cm−1), - CH2 (2874 cm−1), C=O (1657 cm−1), C=N (1557 cm−1), C = C (1440 cm−1), and C – O – C (1010 cm−1). Additionally, the typical ICG absorption peaks locating at 600 -1000 cm−1, 900 - 1100 cm−1, and 1400 - 1500 cm−1, are attributed to C–H out-of-plane bending, C–H vinyl stretching, and aromatic C–C stretching [33]. The out-of-plane bending vibration of isolated hydrogen in the benzene ring of ICG is observed in the absorption band at 890 cm−1 [34] in the sample of CDs-ICG@BSA.

Absorption spectra, excitation spectra, emission spectra, and luminescence lifetimes of CDs and CDs-ICG@BSA have all been measured in order to look into the optical properties of these two types of nanoparticles. ICG containing systems have been found to absorb NIR light and transform it into heat, like copper-based PTA generally do [35]. As indicated in Fig. 1 (a), CDs exhibit an intense absorption band in UV range with the maximum at around 355 nm. Whilst ICG demonstrates one wide and strong band in the range of 650–910 nm, verifying its effective absorption ability in visible and NIR spectral region. However, in CDs-ICG@BSA, two absorption peaks attributed to CDs and ICG respectively are observed. In addition, the broad band in NIR range sees a redshift when compared with that of free ICG, which hints the successful self-assembly of ICG to CDs [32,36,37]. The absorption of CDs-ICG@BSA illustrates that the nanoparticles could be designed as promising PTAs based on the strong NIR absorption. On the other hand, the inset photographs of the samples show that the liquid sample of CDs is colorless and transparent under natural light, while that of ICG has a light green appearance. When ICG is self-assembled to CDs, the color of CDs-ICG@BSA solution turns from colorless to light green. It is also shown that the BSA does not exhibit obvious absorption in the range of 300–1100 nm.

The emission, excitation, and absorption spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 1(b). As an effective luminescent nanomaterial, the synthesized CDs in this study demonstrate an intense blue emission centered at 461 nm with the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 84 nm. The excitation spectrum displays a significant band in the range of 300–400 nm with the maximum at 365 nm, which is consistent with the absorption spectrum as the blue curve shown in this figure. The inserted photographs illustrate that CDs are transparent and colorless in daylight but emit blue light when exposed to 365 nm UV light.

Similarly, the excitation and emission spectra of CDs-ICG@BSA are shown in Fig. 1(c), from which it is deduced that the emission and excitation spectral profiles are analogous with that of CDs except for a slight redshift and broadening of the spectral band. The maximum of the emission of CDs-ICG@BSA located at 465 nm with the FWHM of 86 nm, whilst that for CDs is 461 nm with the FWHM of 84 nm. Correspondingly, the center of the excitation band of CDs-ICG@BSA has redshifted to 378 nm from 365 nm for CDs. The similarity and redshift between CDs-ICG@BSA and CDs illustrate the successful self-assembly of ICG and the basic structure of CDs is maintained in CDs-ICG@BSA samples.

Another crucial characteristic of materials with optical functionality is luminescence lifetime. The luminescence decay curves of CDs and CDs-ICG@BSA are recorded when excited at 365 nm. The luminescence lifetimes of the two carbon quantum dots are depicted in Fig. 1(d). The fitted luminescence lifetimes for CDs and CDs-ICG@BSA are 12.1 ns and 12.6 ns, respectively. This finding suggests that when the CDs are loaded with ICG, the lifetime of the blue carbon quantum is increased, but the change is faint.

It has been reported that BSA is likely to attach to ICG in CDs-ICG, boosting the photothermal effect of CDs-ICG [38]. Therefore, the CDs-ICG system was suspended into BSA solutions with different BSA contents to optimize the concentration of BSA. Fig. 2 (a) and (b) demonstrate the absorption spectra and absorption intensity versus BSA concentration. The strong and wide absorption bands in UV due to CDs and in NIR range originating from ICG are clearly observed in Fig. 2 (a). It is illustrated that the concentration of BSA does not show obvious influence on the absorption of CDs, but it affects the absorption of ICG greatly. With the concentration of BSA increasing, the broad absorption intensity of ICG in CDs-ICG@BSA is enhanced gradually until it reaches the maximum when the concentration of BSA is raised to 5 mg/ml. The inserted photographs are images of CDs-ICG and CDs-ICG@BSA solution after 24 h, which demonstrate that besides enhancing the absorption intensity of ICG, BSA benefits the stability of the nanoparticles as well. As seen in Fig. 2(c), it was found out that the photothermal effect was strengthened as the concentration of BSA was increased because the absorption intensity of ICG was enhanced at the presence of BSA. It is expected that BSA might improve the absorption of ICG in vivo, hence promoting the photothermal effect. It is worth noting that the concentration of ICG in Fig. 2 (c) is fixed at 20 ug/ml, which is very small when compared to the real photothermal treatment. Thus, the temperature of the solution is increased to less than 29 °C when the thermal balance is achieved.

Fig. 2 (a) has verified that BSA does not show obvious influence on the absorption of CD. The emission properties of CDs-ICG@BSA samples have also been investigated as shown in Fig. 3 . The samples exhibit strong and wide exciton emission due to CDs in the range from 420 to 550 nm centered at 463 nm. The emission intensity sees a small decline when the concentration of BSA is increased. But at least, more than 90 % of the initial emission is maintained even though 8 mg/ml of BSA is added to CDs-ICG@BSA. Furthermore, the emission spectra do not show any shift at the presentence of BSA. The normalized emission spectra have proved it. The absorption and emission spectra of CDs-ICG@BSA samples illustrate that BSA enhances the absorption of ICG greatly, might resulting in better phototherapy effect, but BSA does not influence the absorption and the emission of CDs obviously. It means BSA benefits the phototherapy of CDs-ICG@BSA without destroying the bio-imaging application of it.

In addition, the optical stability of CDs-ICG and CDs-ICG@BSA has been studied via emission and absorption spectra. As displayed in Fig. S4 (Supporting Information), the emission of CDs-ICG is slightly increased with time. The possible reason might be the slight decomposition of CDs-ICG composites. On the contrary, the emission and the absorption are relatively stabilized by BSA in CDs-ICG@BSA samples.

CDs-ICG@BSA are employed as PTAs and assessed the photothermal characteristics by their NIR absorption. According to Fig. 4 (a), the UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectra of CDs-ICG@BSA in aqueous solution with various concentrations of ICG demonstrate that the absorption in the NIR region enhances with increasing ICG concentration. The good dispersibility in the aqueous solution was indicated by the intensity of optical absorbance's positive correlation with the ICG content. The extinction coefficient α of CDs-ICG@BSA at the maximum absorption 840 nm could be derived from Lambert-Beer law (A = αbC, where α is the extinction coefficient, b stands for the length of the sample in the wave path, C represents the concentration of CDs-ICG@BSA). As displayed in Fig. S5 (Supporting Information), α was estimated to be 40.2 L g−1 cm−1, which is much greater than that of Ni-CDs (3.23 L g−1 cm−1) [29]. The much bigger extinction coefficient α might be caused by the stronger absorption of ICG organic molecule containing polyaromatic polyene group [38] when compared with that of Ni d-d transition.

The photothermal effect due to ICG depends on its concentration, as shown in Fig. 4 (b) and (c). The temperature of CDs-ICG@BSA solutions ascends quickly, then maintains relatively steady for a long period. Taking CDs-ICG@BSA (the concentration of ICG is 6 mg/ml) as an example, its temperature shoots up to 50.1 °C in 30 s once 808 nm laser is shining on, then the temperature gradually raises to 54.6 °C in 270 s. The CDs-ICG@BSA solutions with less content of ICG exhibit the same tendency, but the increasement of temperature is not so significant as the sample with 6 mg/ml ICG. On the contrary, the photothermal effect of H2O, BSA, CDs, and CDs + BSA under the same experimental set-up is very weak as shown in the bottom of Fig. 4(b). The rise in temperature of these solutions is less than 2 °C in 300 s. The corresponding thermographic images of CDs-ICG@BSA samples with various ICG contents at the beginning of the experiment (t = 0) and t = 300 s are displayed in Fig. 4 (c).

The influence factor of laser power density on photothermal effect is investigated. Different laser power densities (0, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0 W cm−2) were used to irradiate CDs-ICG@BSA. Fig. 4 (d) demonstrates that significant photothermal effect is realized when the laser density is 1.0 W cm−2. CDs-ICG@BSA can therefore function as a potent PTA which converts NIR light into heat. PCE values (Fig. 4 e) were derived from the photothermal cooling curves [15] and the detailed simulation is attached in SI. Notably, the estimated PCE of CDs-ICG@BSA (6 mg/ml) exposed to laser power density of 1 W cm−2 at 808 nm reached up to 61 %, exceeding the majority of the current NIR PTAs as Table 1 shown. The creation of coordination complexes between ICG and CDs, which improves the electron transport efficiency, is responsible for the high PCE of CDs-ICG@BSA.

CDs-ICG@BSA exhibit significant NIR absorption and minimal light scattering because of their ultra-small size and linked nanostructure, which benefits the energy conversion. The following explanations should be given credit for CDs-ICG@BSA good photothermal characteristics. CDs-ICG@BSA have a substantial NIR absorption, which makes it easier to absorb enough NIR photons which might be converted into heat. Their incredibly small size ought to be the second argument. In general, under the same circumstances, smaller nanoparticles have a greater surface area than block samples, which makes them better at converting light into heat [53]. Additionally, smaller nanoparticles exhibit less light scattering, resulting in less light loss when exposed to light. With minimal light loss during irradiation, CDs-ICG@BSA with extremely small particles may effectively convert NIR light into heat. Thirdly, CDs-ICG@BSA have good solubility and dispersion in water, which enables them to quickly transmit heat to the surrounding media, raising the temperature of that medium. Even after six heating-cooling cycles, the photothermal characteristics of CDs-ICG@BSA did not significantly alter (Fig. 4(f)), demonstrating their high photothermal stability.

CDs-ICG@BSA are promising reagents for cancer bioimaging and photothermal therapy due to their exceptional PCE, strong photostability, and great dispersion in water. In order to determine the cytotoxicity of CDs-ICG@BSA before employing them as a PTA for phototherapy treatment and cancer bioimaging, 4T1 cells are used to perform an MTT experiment. The findings demonstrate that the cell survival rate is greater than 95 % after 24 h of incubation with CD concentrations from 40 to 240 μg/ml (Figure S6 a). Also, it is worth noting that the toxicity of CDs-ICG@BSA to 4T1 cells was negligible even when ICG concentrations reaches as high as 600 μg/ml, as shown in Fig. S6 (b). The proved low toxicity should be due to non-toxicity of ICG and CDs, which also demonstrates the high biocompatibility of CDs-ICG@BSA.

Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (CLSM) was used to analyze the intracellular uptake of CDs-ICG@BSA in order to explore cell imaging, as illustrated in Fig. 5 . After the CDs-ICG@BSA nanoparticles were co-cultured with 4T1 cells, blue and red luminescence were observed and the shape of the cell was well maintained upon excitation with 408 nm and 640 nm, respectively. This indicates that CDs-ICG@BSA was primarily found in the cell membrane and cytoplasm but did not enter the nucleolus. It shows that CDs-ICG@BSA could be employed as cellular bioimaging agents. Notably, the CDs-ICG@BSA nanoparticles may be excited with dual excitation wavelengths and emerge two different colors with the cells keeping good morphology. It illustrates that the CDs-ICG@BSA nanoparticles possess good biocompatibility and excellent luminescence. This results further supports the advantages of CDs-ICG@BSA applied in bio-imaging.

Based on the bioimaging of CDs-ICG@BSA samples, the phototherapy could be carried out in situ with the monitor of imaging. 4T1 cells were incubated with CDs-ICG@BSA nanoparticles for 24 h at 37 °C, and then were exposed to an 808 nm laser with a power density of 1 W cm−2 for 10 min in order to assess the therapeutic effect of CDs-ICG@BSA against 4T1 cells. Samples displayed no evident cytotoxicity without NIR irradiation, as seen in Fig. 6 (a). More than 95 % of 4T1 cells were active even when the concentration of CDs-ICG@BSA reached 300 μg/ml. However, the survival percentage of 4T1 cells fell to only 15 % if 808 nm laser was radiated on the samples, showing that 4T1 cells had suffered serious damage from NIR radiation in 4T1 cells incubated with CDs-ICG@BSA.

It is known that ICG has both PDT and PTT effects [21,22]. To further investigate the main mechanism of the cell injury in this work, the effect of PDT or PTT is distinguished. Accordingly, vitamin C (Vc) was added as a ROS scavenger to eliminate the PDT effect, and the ice bath treatment was carried out to eliminate the PTT effect. Specifically, the cells were pre-treated with 25 mM Vc for 2 h prior to irradiation of an 808 nm laser. The cell survival results (Fig. 6 b) demonstrate PTT effect. By contrast, ice water bath was employed during the irradiation process to dissipate heat. The obtained cell survival results (Fig. 6 c) are caused by PDT effect.

The phototoxicity results (Fig. 6 a) showed that when the concentration of CDs-ICG@BSA was 300 μg/mL, most of the tumor cells were injured by PDT and PTT effects, ∼15 % of the initial cells were survived. Whilst, ∼20 % of the tumor cells were survival under PTT effect, which approached the results of PDT and PTT effects (∼15 %). However, PDT effect demonstrated a poor performance in killing tumor cells (more than ∼58 % survived). These findings reveal that the synthesized CDs-ICG@BSA nanoparticles have remarkable PTT/PDT combined effect on 4T1 cells, but PTT played the predominant role. The effective conversion of NIR light to heat owing to ICG is the key contribution to cell injury [54].

The in-situ cell imaging could be achieved by CDs-ICG@BSA nanoparticles. Live cells were labeled with green fluorescence by Calcein-AM and dead cells were stained with red fluorescence by propidium iodide to investigate the PTT impact of the materials. Fig. 6 (d) and (e) show the confocal images of 4T1 cells. They revealed that 4T1 cells treated with CDs-ICG@BSA samples did not experience any cell damage prior to being exposed to 808 nm laser, but they were perished after exposure to NIR laser because of the phototherapy caused by CDs-ICG@BSA in the samples. All of these findings demonstrate that CDs-ICG@BSA have good phototherapy and in situ bioimaging functions.

In summary, CDs-ICG@BSA nanoparticles with low toxicity, excellent photothermal therapy and bioimaging abilities have been synthesized. They also have good water solubility and dispersibility, strong photostability, homogeneous distribution, and are extremely small in size. Notably, because of their excellent luminescent properties, biocompatibility and low cellular toxicity, CDs-ICG@BSA can be employed for bioimaging at dual excitation wavelengths. Additionally, CDs-ICG@BSA exhibits an efficient potential for tumor treatment and can be eliminated from the body through metabolic pathways, hence reducing its long-term biotoxicity. The results of in vitro cellular studies demonstrate that CDs-ICG@BSA have acceptable photothermal antitumor effectiveness when exposed to 808 nm laser irradiation. The distinctive CDs-ICG@BSA have demonstrated that they are a viable therapeutic diagnostic system in cancer therapy due to their large NIR absorption, high PCE, bioimaging at dual excitation wavelengths, and strong photothermal/photodynamic effects. This straightforward approach may pave the way for the development of multifunctional photothermal agents for effective phototherapy treatment of tumors.

Xianju Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Qingchi Cai: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shouchun Zhao: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Faling Ling: Software, Formal analysis. Guotao Xiang: Project administration, Investigation, Formal analysis. Li Li: Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yongjie Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yanhong Li: Methodology, Data curation. Xiao Tang: Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (11704054,12004062); Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (KJZD-M202000601, KJQN202100615), and the construction of double city economic circle in Chengdu Chongqing region scientific and technological innovation project (KJCX2020028).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.125661.
